ocr.text: In the context of hematological assessments, the relationship between various analytes and the presence of splenomegaly is
crucial for accurate diagnosis and treatment planning.
Analytes  Warning
aPTT  Test executed in the last 24h
VANCO  Test executed in the last 72h
Blood count  Test executed in the last 24h
CRP  Test executed in the last 72h
PT  Test executed in the last 24h
FERRI  This test should be repeated not before 3 months or as suggested by a specialist
TSH  This test should be repeated not before 6 months or as suggested by a specialist
The data presented in the table elucidates the distribution of specific analytes among patients with and without splenomegaly,
highlighting significant variations in clinical outcomes. Notably, the column labeled “Medication (n, % of total)” provides
insight into the pharmacotherapy status of the patient cohorts, revealing that a substantial proportion of the patients received no
pharmacological intervention prior to testing.
Medication (n, % of total)  With splenomegaly (n=102)  Without splenomegaly (n=78)
No pharmacotherapy  42 (41)  44 (56)
Any pharmacotherapy  60 (59)  34 (44)
Hydroxyurea  36 (35)  19 (24)
Growth factors  23 (23)  10 (13)
Thalidomide  17 (17)  2 (3)
Lenalidomide  2 (2)  4 (5)
Interferon-α  12 (12)  3 (4)
Anagrelide  8 (8)  2 (3)
This absence of medication is particularly relevant in understanding the baseline coagulation profiles, as indicated by the
activated partial thromboplastin time (aPTT) values, which were executed within the last 24 hours. Furthermore, the “With
splenomegaly (n=102)” and “Without splenomegaly (n=78)” columns facilitate a comparative analysis that underscores the
potential impact of splenic enlargement on hemostatic function. By integrating these findings, clinicians can better interpret the
implications of splenomegaly on patient management and treatment strategies, ultimately enhancing the quality of care
provided to those affected by hematological disorders.
